title-group.article-title: Proteomic Analysis of 442 Clinical Plasma Samples From Individuals With Symptom Records Revealed Subtypes of Convalescent Patients Who Had COVID‐19

author-notes.corresp: * Correspondence: Jiangfeng Liu (ljf@pumc.edu.cn) Lili Ren (renliliipb@163.com) Juntao Yang (yangjt@pumc.edu.cn)

permissions.copyright-statement: Journal of Medical Virology© 2025 The Author(s). Journal of Medical Virology published by Wiley Periodicals LLC.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: After the coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19) pandemic, the postacute effects of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2) infection have gradually attracted attention. To precisely evaluate the health status of convalescent patients with COVID‐19, we analyzed symptom and proteome data of 442 plasma samples from healthy controls, hospitalized patients, and convalescent patients 6 or 12 months after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. Symptoms analysis revealed distinct relationships in convalescent patients. Results of plasma protein expression levels showed that C1QA, C1QB, C2, CFH, CFHR1, and F10, which regulate the complement system and coagulation, remained highly expressed even at the 12‐month follow‐up compared with their levels in healthy individuals. By combining symptom and proteome data, 442 plasma samples were categorized into three subtypes: S1 (metabolism‐healthy), S2 (COVID‐19 retention), and S3 (long COVID). We speculated that convalescent patients reporting hair loss could have a better health status than those experiencing headaches and dyspnea. Compared to other convalescent patients, those reporting sleep disorders, appetite decrease, and muscle weakness may need more attention because they were classified into the S2 subtype, which had the most samples from hospitalized patients with COVID‐19. Subtyping convalescent patients with COVID‐19 may enable personalized treatments tailored to individual needs. This study provides valuable plasma proteomic datasets for further studies associated with long COVID.

award-group.funding-source: This work was supported by grants from the National Key R&D Program of China (2023YFC2507102), the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences Innovation Fund for Medical Sciences, China (CIFMS2022‐I2M‐1‐011, CIFMS2022‐I2M‐2‐001, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐057, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐049, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐044, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐016, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐001, 2022‐I2M‐CoV19‐003, and CIFMS2022‐I2M‐JB‐003), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82341064), the Haihe Laboratory of Cell Ecosystem Innovation Fund (22HHXBSS00008 and 22HHKYZX0034), and State Key Laboratory Special Fund 2060204.

fn.p: Jiangfeng Liu, Li Guo, Jingchuan Zhong, and Yue Wu contributed equally to this study.

def.p: Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Several years have passed since the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19). The concept of “long COVID,” a postacute syndrome of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2), has gradually attracted attention. Convalescent patients with COVID‐19 often report multiple symptoms, including postexertional malaise, fatigue, muscle weakness, dizziness, sleep difficulties, anxiety, depression, gastrointestinal symptoms, palpitations, loss of smell or taste, thirst, chronic cough, chest pain, and abnormal movements [1, 2, 3]. These persistent symptoms suggest that convalescent patients with COVID‐19 potentially require ongoing medical support.

Symptoms are defined as reports from patients, whereas signs are defined as metrics measured by medical professionals. Similar signs or clinical test results may be described differently for various reasons. Therefore, symptoms are not the only reference for clinical decisions. As technology develops, broad and unbiased proteomic data have been applied to distinguish therapeutic methods in patients with similar clinical manifestations. Using proteomic subtyping, Jiang et al. [4] identified hepatocellular carcinoma with poor prognosis at an early stage and suggested SOAT1 as a novel therapeutic target. Since 2011, the National Cancer Institute's Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium has generated genomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, and clinical data for over 1000 tumors [5]. Proteomics often identifies molecular tumor phenotypes that cannot be distinguished using conventional clinical detection methods, providing novel diagnostic and therapeutic perspectives [5]. Proteome‐based investigations have also been used for many other diseases, including COVID‐19 [6, 7, 8, 9, 10]. In the early days of COVID‐19, proteomics combined with machine learning was used to detect SARS‐CoV‐2 [11, 12], explore infection mechanisms [9, 13, 14, 15, 16], evaluate disease severity in just‐infected patients [6, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22], and identify potential therapeutic targets [23, 24, 25].

Proteomics and other omic technologies are gradually applied to study post‐COVID‐19 manifestation, including long COVID studies [26, 27, 28, 29, 30]. Gu et al. [31] compared plasma protein profiling between healthy individuals and convalescent patients with COVID‐19, introducing proteins related to each symptom. Klein et al. [32] separated individuals with or without long COVID symptoms, reporting different circulating myeloid and lymphocyte populations and exaggerated humoral responses against SARS‐CoV‐2 among participants with long COVID. Liew et al. [33] distinguished individuals with at least one long COVID symptom and those fully recovered, identifying plasma proteins associated with long COVID, especially those linked to cardiorespiratory symptoms, fatigue, anxiety/depression, gastrointestinal symptoms, and cognitive impairment. Combined with machine learning, plasma proteins were used to study different phenotypes and predict the severity of long COVID [34, 35]. However, studies rarely used both clinical symptom and plasma protein data to distinguish subtypes of convalescent patients with COVID‐19. Using reported symptoms alone to define long COVID, Bodansky et al. [36] identified a distinct autoreactive signature that separated individuals with prior SARS‐CoV‐2 infection from those never exposed to the virus. Regardless, autoreactivity patterns that separated individuals with long COVID from those who fully recovered were not detected. The inconsistencies between patient‐reported symptoms and detected autoreactive signatures underscore the need for additional metrics to evaluate long COVID.

We hypothesized that combining subjective symptoms with liquid chromatography‐mass spectrometry (LC‐MS)‐measured plasma protein expression levels offer independent objective metrics that contribute to the selection of suitable treatments for different convalescent patients. Therefore, to test this hypothesis, COVID‐19‐associated clinical plasma samples were collected for proteome analysis, which supplements postinfection symptom and long COVID studies.

sec.title: Questionnaire Investigation, Sample Collection, and Ethics
sec.p: The plasma samples used in this study were obtained from reported cohorts at Jin Yin‐tan Hospital (Wuhan, China) [1, 3]. The participants included healthy individuals (healthy controls, HC group), hospitalized patients (HO group), convalescent patients at the 6‐month follow‐up (first follow‐up, F1 group), and convalescent patients at the 12‐month follow‐up (second follow‐up, F2 group). Healthy individuals were selected from among those who had never been infected with SARS‐CoV‐2 and had normal health status. Convalescent patients were interviewed using a series of questionnaires to evaluate their symptoms and health status. Plasma samples were collected during blood tests. All collected plasma samples were heated at 56°C for 30 min for virus inactivation, then stored at −80°C until use. This study was approved by the Research Ethics Commission of Jin Yin‐tan Hospital (KY‐2020‐78.01, KY‐2020‐78.03). Written informed consent was obtained from all participants.

sec.title: Evaluation of Relationship Among Symptoms
sec.p: COVID‐19 symptoms were recorded using graded numbers. At the 6‐ or 12‐month follow‐up, Spearman's correlation coefficients were calculated for symptom grade, disease level, and age, and the threshold was set at p < 0.01 with a correlation coefficient > 0.3. Significantly correlated symptoms were classified into single symptom clusters. For each patient, the symptom grades in a cluster were summed. Summed symptom grades were then used to evaluate the relationships between protein expression values, using Spearman's correlation coefficient, setting a significant threshold at p < 0.05.

sec.title: Protein Extraction
sec.p: Frozen plasma samples were melted on ice and centrifuged at 12 000×g at 4°C for 10 min. The supernatant of each sample was collected, and protein concentration was determined using a BCA kit (Beyotime) according to the manufacturer's instructions.

To prepare a spectral library, equal amounts of protein were extracted from each sample and mixed. Highly abundant proteins in mixed samples were removed using a Pierce Top 14 Abundant Protein Depletion Spin Column. Other samples did not undergo this high‐abundance protein depletion process.

sec.title: Trypsin Digestion
sec.p: Equal amounts of protein were extracted from each sample and diluted with 8M urea solution (Sigma‐Aldrich). Proteins in the mixed samples prepared for spectral library construction were subjected to trypsin digestion. Protein solution was reduced with 5 mM dithiothreitol (Sigma‐Aldrich) at 56°C for 30 min and alkylated with 11 mM iodoacetamide (Sigma‐Aldrich) at room temperature in darkness for 15 min. The alkylated samples were transferred to ultrafiltration tubes for filter‐aided sample preparation. Samples were replaced with 8 M urea solution using centrifugation at 12 000 × g for 20 min at room temperature three times and then replaced with 200 mM triethylammonium bicarbonate three times. Trypsin (Promega) was added at 1:50 trypsin‐to‐protein mass ratio for digestion overnight. The peptides were recovered using centrifugation at 12 000 × g for 10 min at room temperature, which was repeated twice. The combined peptides were desalted using a Strata X SPE column.

sec.title: High‐Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) Fractionation
sec.p: Peptides in the mixed sample for spectral library construction were fractionated using high pH reverse‐phase HPLC with an Agilent 300Extend C18 column (5 μm particles, 4.6 mm ID, 250 mm length). The chromatographic column was pretreated with 95% buffer A (0.1% formic acid and 2% acetonitrile in water) for 30 min at 35°C. Peptides in the mixed samples were loaded onto the HPLC system, and the separated samples were collected every 1 min. Fractions 11 to 46 were combined into 12 fractions and dried using vacuum centrifugation.

sec.title: LC‐MS/MS Analysis
sec.p: Construction of the spectral library: Fractionated peptides in the mixed sample were dissolved in solvent A (0.1% formic acid and 2% acetonitrile in water) and then loaded onto a homemade reverse‐phase analytical column (100 μm ID, 250 mm length) equipped with an EASY‐nLC 1200 ultra performance liquid chromatography (UPLC) system (ThermoFisher Scientific). The mobile phase consisted of solvents A and B (0.1% formic acid and 90% acetonitrile in water). Peptides were separated using the following gradient: 0–60 min, 4%–24% B; 60–69 min, 24%–32% B; 69–72 min, 32%–80% B; 72%–75 min, 80% B. A constant flow rate of 500 nL/min was used. Separated peptides were analyzed using an Orbitrap Exploris 480 mass spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific) equipped with a nanoelectrospray ion source. Data‐dependent analysis (DDA) mode was used for data acquisition.

Data‐independent analysis (DIA): Peptides from each sample were separated using the EASY‐nLC 1200 UPLC system under the same separation conditions as the peptides in the mixed sample. The separated peptides were analyzed using the same Orbitrap Exploris 480 mass spectrometer, and the precursors and fragments were analyzed using the Orbitrap detector. The full MS scan was performed with a resolution set to 60 000 for a scan range of 400–1200 m/z. The MS/MS scan resolution was set to 15 000. The DIA mode was used for data acquisition. After the primary scan, peptide segment ions with 20 m/z windows were transferred to the high energy collision dissociation collision pool for fragmentation with 28% fragmentation energy, and secondary mass spectrometry was performed. The automatic gain control of the secondary spectrum was set to 1000%.

sec.title: Database Search
sec.p: The 12 raw files obtained in the DDA mode were imported into the Spectronaut 16 software for spectral library construction. Homo Sapiens protein sequences were downloaded from UniProt (https://www.uniprot.org/), and 20 407 sequences were downloaded on March 7, 2023. Trypsin was used as the digestion enzyme. The maximum number of missed cleavages was set to one. Carbamidomethyl (C) was used as a fixed modification. Oxidation (M) and acetylation (Protein N‐term) were used as variable modifications. False discovery rate (FDR) of the peptides and proteins was set at < 0.01.

The 442 raw files obtained in DIA mode were imported into Spectronaut 16, using the same protein sequences employed for spectral library construction. Retention time prediction type was set to dynamic indexed retention time. Interference of the MS2 level correction was enabled. Cross‐run normalization was performed as required. The Q‐value cutoff was set at < 0.01.

sec.title: Proteomic Data Management
sec.p: Proteins with an intensity of < 1 in any sample were excluded. Proteins present in all samples were selected, and the median intensities of these proteins in each sample were calculated. The normalization of protein intensity in each sample was calculated as ([intensity of each protein]/[median intensity of shared proteins]) × (maximum median intensity among all samples). Shared proteins indicate proteins identified in all samples. After log2 transformation, proteins with missing values in > 50% of the samples were excluded. Missing values for the remaining proteins were imputed using the lowest matrix values.

sec.title: Functional Enrichment Analysis
sec.p:
The Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database was used to annotate proteins. Functional enrichment analysis was performed using over‐representation analysis, which is a statistical significance test based on a hypergeometric distribution.
P=∑Mi=mMiN−Mn−iNn=1−∑m−1i=0MiN−Mn−iNn.

In the above formula: “N” indicates the count of all annotated genes in KEGG database, “M” indicates the count of annotated genes in a specific pathway in KEGG database, “n” indicates the count of all annotated genes in the input protein data set, and “m” indicates the count of annotated genes in the specific pathway in the input protein data set. Enrichment threshold was set at p < 0.05. Pathways with fewer than two proteins were excluded from the analysis.

sec.title: Trends in Protein Alteration Relative to COVID‐19 Severity
sec.p: Samples from the HC and HO groups were exclusively used to study the correlation between protein expression levels and COVID‐19 severity. Median value of each protein was calculated for HC, moderate, severe, and critical individuals. Fuzzy c‐means algorithm was used to classify the proteins [37]. The fuzzy c‐means algorithm was invoked from the Mfuzz module integrated into https://www.bioladder.cn/. Proteins that had a coefficient of variation (CV) ≤ 0.1 among HC, moderate, severe, and critical samples were excluded, and the other parameters were set as default. Subsequently, 177 proteins remained, which were classified into 12 clusters. Based on the protein expression alteration trends, the proteins in clusters 3, 11, and 12 were defined as ascending, whereas those in clusters 2, 4, and 9 were defined as descending.

sec.title: Trends in Protein Alterations Over Time Following COVID‐19
sec.p: All samples were used to study the correlation between protein expression levels since COVID‐19 infection. Student's t‐test was used to compare protein expression levels between the HO and HC groups. By setting an FDR threshold of < 0.05 along with fold change (FC) > 1.5 (log2FC > 0.585) or FC < 0.67 (log2FC < −0.585), 62 proteins were identified. The median expression value of each remaining protein was then calculated for HC, HO, F1, and F2 groups. Without filtering CV, the fuzzy c‐means algorithm was used to classify the proteins. The parameters were set to default values, and the 62 proteins were classified into 12 clusters. According to the protein alteration trends, proteins in clusters 2, 9, and 11 were defined as reversibly upregulated; proteins in cluster 8 were defined as reversibly downregulated; proteins in clusters 3 and 5 were defined as irreversibly upregulated; and proteins in clusters 1, 4, 6, 7, 10, and 12 were defined as irreversibly downregulated.

sec.title: Non‐Negative Matrix Factorization (NMF)‐Based Subtyping of Plasma Samples Using Protein Expression Values
sec.p: Proteins with the top 30% CV among the samples were imported into the NMF R package (V0.26, method: brunet) for NMF subtyping [38].

sec.title: Selection of Subtype Signature Proteins
sec.p: Subtype signature proteins were selected based on two criteria: the protein was expressed in > 70% of samples in at least one subtype, and the protein was differentially expressed between one subtype and the others. Differential analysis was performed using a simple linear model and moderated t‐statistics implemented in the R/Bioconductor limma package (V3.50.0). Proteins with p < 0.05 and FC > 1.2 (log2FC > 0.263) were defined as upregulated subtype signature proteins, while proteins with p < 0.05 and FC < 0.83 (log2FC < −0.263) were defined as downregulated subtype signature proteins.

sec.title: Other Statistical Analysis
sec.p: R software (V4.1.2) was used for multiple statistical analyses. To evaluate symptom differences among the subtypes, Fisher's exact test was used to analyze modified British Medical Research Council (mMRC) scale, appetite, anxiety, myalgia, nausea, pain, and taste scores, while the Chi‐square test was used for other symptoms. The threshold for significance was set at p < 0.05. The Chi‐squared test was invoked using the chisq.test function, and Fisher's exact test was invoked using the fisher.test function, both from the stats package in R. A Circos plot was constructed using the circlize (V0.4.14) R package, and heatmaps were generated using the pheatmap (V1.0.12) R package.

sec.title: Participant Information and Their Symptom Records
sec.p: Plasma samples were collected from 22 healthy controls (HC group), 43 hospitalized patients (HO group), 196 convalescent patients 6 months after COVID‐19 infection (first follow‐up, F1 group), and 181 convalescent patients 12 months after COVID‐19 infection (second follow‐up, F2 group). Healthy controls were selected from people who had never been infected with SARS‐CoV‐2 and had a normal health status. The convalescent patients completed a questionnaire‐based survey of their clinical symptoms. For convalescent patients, 19 symptoms, including fatigue, headache, myalgia, sore throat, palpitations, dizziness, skin rash, nausea, smell loss, taste loss, appetite decrease, sleep, hair loss, dyspnea (measured using the mMRC scale), and anxiety, were recorded using graded numbers (Supporting Information S4: Table S1). At the first follow‐up, hair loss, fatigue, dyspnea, sleep disorders, muscle weakness, and anxiety were recorded. At the second follow‐up, hair loss, fatigue, appetite decrease, joint pain, and dyspnea were the most commonly reported symptoms (Table 1). Compared to those at the first follow‐up, reports of hair loss decreased at the second follow‐up, whereas complaints of headache, myalgia, chest pain, sore throat, palpitations, dizziness, skin rash, and joint pain increased among individuals (Table 1). A Circos plot directly presents the complexity of symptom transformation from the first to the second follow‐up (Figure 1 and Supporting Information S5: Table S2). We determined minimal overlap in convalescent patients who reported hair loss between the first and second follow‐ups (Figure 1). After preliminary symptom analysis, plasma samples from all participants were processed for proteomic detection to collect objective metrics for convalescent patients with COVID‐19 (Figure 2).

caption.p: Statistical analysis results of symptoms from clinical records.

Symptom transformation during coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19) recovery. Transformation of symptoms from the 6‐month follow‐up (F1, first follow‐up) to the 12‐month follow‐up (F2, second follow‐up) during COVID‐19 recovery. Further details are provided in Supporting Information S5: Table S2.
Workflow chart. Workflow showing the grouping of participants, questionnaire‐based symptom records, and plasma proteome detection using liquid chromatography‐mass spectrometry (LC‐MS).

sec.title: Plasma Proteome Profiling of 442 Participants
sec.p: A total of 1285 plasma proteins were identified (Supporting Information S1: Figure S1A and Supporting Information S6: Table S3). The distribution of protein intensity was consistent among the samples (Supporting Information S1: Figure S1B), revealing reliable quality for subsequent analyses. For preliminary distinction, principal component analysis (PCA) was used to perform unsupervised clustering of the identified proteins. Using all 1285 proteins in the calculation, the PCA plots of the samples from the HC, HO, F1, and F2 groups overlapped. However, each group exhibited a different distribution pattern. For example, the proteomic features of patients in the HC, HO, F1, and F2 groups were increasingly discrete (Supporting Information S1: Figure S1C).

Building on studies that link plasma proteins and COVID‐19 severity, this study explored protein expression trends corresponding to COVID‐19 severity in hospitalized patients. For samples from the HC and HO groups, COVID‐19 severity was classified into four levels: healthy, moderate, severe, and critical. Using the fuzzy c‐means algorithm, we identified 12 clusters of protein expression trends corresponding to COVID‐19 severity (Supporting Information S2: Figure S2A and Supporting Information S7: Table S4). The protein expression levels of the three clusters increased (defined as ascending and indicated by red frames) as COVID‐19 severity worsened, whereas the expression levels of another three clusters decreased (defined as descending and indicated by blue frames). Proteins in the ascending clusters were merged for KEGG pathway enrichment analysis, as were proteins in the descending clusters. Ascending proteins were mainly associated with the complement and coagulation cascades (Supporting Information S2: Figure S2B and Supporting Information S7: Table S4), whereas descending proteins were associated with cell adhesion pathways (Supporting Information S2: Figure S2C and Supporting Information S7: Table S4).

sec.title: Relationships Between Plasma Proteins, Symptoms, and Recovery Time
sec.p: The potential relationships between proteins and questionnaire‐recorded symptoms were explored. Some convalescent patients reported multiple symptoms at one follow‐up (Supporting Information S4: Table S1), indicating a relationship between the symptoms. We used graded numbers to represent sex (female: 1, male: 2) and COVID‐19 severity (moderate: 1, severe: 2, critical: 3). Spearman's correlation coefficients between symptoms, age, sex, and disease level (COVID‐19 severity) were calculated, and the cutoff value was set at p < 0.01. Symptoms with Spearman's correlation coefficients > 0.3 were classified into one cluster. At the first follow‐up, pain and anxiety were positively correlated (symptom cluster 1), whereas fatigue, smell loss, taste loss, decreased appetite, sleep disorders, and muscle weakness tended to occur simultaneously (symptom cluster 2; Figure 3 and Supporting Information S8: Table S5). At the second follow‐up, a syndrome containing 12 positively correlated elements (age, fatigue, myalgia, chest pain, palpitations, taste loss, appetite decrease, sleep disorders, muscle weakness, joint pain, dyspnea, pain, and anxiety) was identified (symptom cluster 3; Figure 3 and Supporting Information S9: Table S6). In each symptom cluster, the graded numbers of each symptom were summed for each patient, and the collective results were used to calculate Spearman's correlation coefficients between the symptom cluster and plasma protein expression levels. The symptom cluster‐related proteins were filtered for KEGG enrichment analysis. In significantly enriched KEGG pathways (p < 0.05), 14 proteins, including PRKACA, MEGF8, PPM1A, FLT4, DUSP3, TAOK3, SERPINA7, GPX1, CTSD, CLTC, FUCA2, GNPTG, ACE, and KRT16 were positively related to anxiety and pain (symptom cluster 1); PRNP, LAMC1, STIP1, and PRKCD were positively correlated with symptom cluster 2, whereas HLA‐A, MADCAM1, ITGB1, ATP2A2, UBB, and ANPEP were negatively related with this cluster (Figure 3); and STX7, STX11, EGFR, GNAI2, CACNA2D1, and PPP1CA were negatively correlated with symptom cluster 3 (Figure 3).

caption.p: Significantly correlated symptoms and proteins at the first follow‐up (F1 group) or the second follow‐up (F2 group). The left panel shows the significantly correlated symptoms. Spearman's correlation coefficients were calculated for symptom grade, disease level, and age, and the threshold was set at p < 0.01. Red lines represent positive correlations, and blue lines represent negative correlations. The thicker the lines, the higher the absolute values of the correlation coefficients. At least one correlation coefficient between symptoms was > 0.3 in the red dotted circles (symptom clusters 1, 2, and 3). The right panel shows proteins that were significantly correlated with symptom clusters 1, 2, or 3. The symptom grades of one patient in each symptom cluster were summed. Spearman's correlation coefficients were calculated between summed symptom grades and protein expression values, and proteins with p < 0.05 were used for functional enrichment analysis. Further details are provided in Supporting Information S8: Table S5 and Supporting Information S9: Table S6.

sec.p: Recovery time‐related protein expression trends were identified. Among the differentially expressed proteins between the HC and HO groups, 12 clusters of expression trends containing 62 proteins were identified in the HC, HO, F1, and F2 samples (Figure 4 and Supporting Information S10: Table S7). We classified protein expression trends into four types: reversibly upregulated, reversibly downregulated, irreversibly upregulated, and irreversibly downregulated. Reversibly upregulated (clusters 2, 9, and 11; Figure 4) proteins were increasingly expressed during hospitalization but had similar expression levels between HC and follow‐up samples. Irreversibly upregulated (clusters 3 and 5; Figure 4) proteins showed increased expression levels in all HO, F1, and F2 samples compared with those in HC samples. Reversibly downregulated proteins (cluster 8; Figure 4) and irreversibly downregulated proteins (clusters 1, 4, 6, 7, 10, and 12; Figure 4) were defined as reversibly and irreversibly upregulated proteins, respectively. Among the irreversibly upregulated proteins, we identified IGLV8‐61 and IGLV3‐27, which are immunoglobulins; AGT, an angiotensinogen; C1QA, C1QB, C2, CFH, CFHR1, and F10, which regulate the complement system and coagulation; SERPINF1, which inhibits angiogenesis; AMBP, APOH, HPX, and A1BG, which are glycoproteins; and EFEMP1 and COL18A1, which are extracellular matrix proteins (Figure 4).

caption.p: Plasma protein expression trends considering recovery time after coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19). In total, 442 samples were used in this study. Differentially expressed proteins (Student's t‐test, p < 0.05 with FCHO/HC > 1.5 or FCHO/HC < 0.67) between the HC and HO groups were kept. These differentially expressed proteins were then separated into 12 clusters using the fuzzy c‐means algorithm, based on protein expression trends among the HC, HO, F1, and F2 groups. Reversibly upregulated clusters are indicated with red frames. The reversibly downregulated cluster is indicated with a blue frame. Irreversibly upregulated clusters are indicated with red dotted frames. Irreversibly downregulated clusters are indicated with blue dotted frames. Further details are provided in Supporting Information S10: Table S7. F1, first follow‐up group; F2, second follow‐up group; HC, healthy control group; HO, hospitalized patient group.

sec.title: Plasma Protein Expression Modes Categorized Convalescent Patients Into Three Subtypes
sec.p: Based on the protein expression levels, we used NMF, an unsupervised clustering method, and separated all 442 plasma samples into three subtypes (S1, S2, and S3; Supporting Information S3: Figure S3 and Supporting Information S11: Table S8). The signature proteins of each subtype were calculated (Supporting Information S12: Table S9) and presented as a heat map. The plasma sample groups and all recorded symptoms are marked above the signature proteins (Figure 5). Between the plasma sample groups and NMF subtypes, we found that S1 subtype contained mainly HC samples, S2 subtype contained mainly HO samples, and S3 subtype contained only F1 and F2 samples. Annotation of the signature proteins (Supporting Information S12: Table S9) showed that the S1 subtype upregulated metabolism‐associated proteins, S2 subtype upregulated surfactant metabolism‐associated proteins (including SFTPB), and S3 subtype downregulated neuronal function‐associated proteins (including NRGN). To evaluate symptom differences among the subtypes, Fisher's exact test was used for mMRC, appetite, anxiety, myalgia, nausea, pain, and taste scores, while the Chi‐square test was used for other symptoms. The threshold was set at p < 0.05. Six symptoms, including dyspnea (mMRC), headache, muscle weakness, decreased appetite, sleep disorders, and hair loss, were significantly different among subtypes S1, S2, and S3 (Figure 6 and Supporting Information S13: Table S10). The S1 subtype had the highest incidence of hair loss; S2 subtype had the highest incidence of sleep disorders, appetite decrease, and muscle weakness; and S3 subtype had the highest incidence of headache and dyspnea (mMRC). Downregulated neuronal function‐associated proteins may explain why convalescent patients with the S3 subtype frequently had headaches.

caption.p: Subtypes of convalescent patients with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19) based on plasma proteome data. The plasma samples were classified into three subtypes (S1, S2, and S3) using the NMF algorithm. Sample groups, symptoms (upper panel), and expression values of subtype signature proteins (lower panel; see Supporting Information S12: Table S9) are presented as heat maps. NMF, non‐negative matrix factorization.

Different symptom characteristics of convalescent patients with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19) in each subtype. Six symptoms were significantly different among subtypes S1, S2, and S3. Fisher's exact test was used to compare the incidence of mMRC and appetite in subtypes S1, S2, and S3, and the Chi‐square test was used to compare the other symptoms. The thresholds was set at p < 0.05. Further details are provided in Supporting Information S13: Table S10. mMRC, modified British Medical Research Council scale.

sec.p: Collectively, we propose naming the S1 subtype as a metabolism‐healthy subtype (containing upregulated metabolism‐associated proteins, significant hair loss symptoms, and most HC samples), S2 subtype as a COVID‐19 retention subtype (containing upregulated surfactant metabolism‐associated proteins, most HO samples, significant sleep disorders, decreased appetite, and muscle weakness symptoms), and S3 subtype as a long COVID subtype (containing plasma samples exclusively from convalescent patients, significant headache and dyspnea symptoms, and downregulated neuronal function‐associated proteins).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this study, we combined the subjective symptoms and objective plasma protein expression levels to explore the postacute effects of SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. Plasma samples from healthy individuals, hospitalized patients with COVID‐19, and convalescent patients with COVID‐19 were separated into S1 (metabolism‐healthy), S2 (COVID‐19 retention), and S3 (long COVID) subtypes. We speculated that general nursing or psychological interventions were suitable for convalescent patients with S1 subtype, whereas convalescent patients with subtypes S2 and S3 required more medical surveillance and interventions. Convalescent patients who reported hair loss may have a better health status than those who reported headaches and dyspnea. Compared to other convalescent patients, those who reported sleep disorders, appetite decrease, and muscle weakness may require more attention because they were classified into the S2 subtype, which had the most samples from hospitalized patients with COVID‐19.

The key highlights of this study include: (1) inclusion of participants who had a single SARS‐CoV‐2 infection; (2) analysis of two‐time points (6 and 12 months) postinfection; (3) identification of symptom clusters; and (4) combination of plasma proteins and symptoms to identify convalescent patient subtypes.

The estimated prevalence of long COVID varies across statistical reports. Besides the duration and population, Brightling and Evans [39] commented that the confusion of long COVID symptoms and pre‐existent disease symptoms could contribute to biased long COVID prevalence. Captur et al. [29] indicated that people who would get long COVID have characteristic plasma proteomic signatures even early after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. Using plasma samples collected within 6 weeks after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection and a targeted proteome analysis including 91 proteins, HSCB, HSP90ABI, APP, PLD3, CST3, and S100‐A9 at seroconversion were predicted to be strongly associated with persistent symptoms lasting up to 12 months [29]. Our study collected plasma samples up to 12 months after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection and identified over 1000 proteins, providing datasets for long COVID studies. Furthermore, we identified notable roles for the complement system in COVID‐19. Previous reports have shown that complements are hyperactive during SARS‐CoV‐2 infection [40, 41], may distinguish COVID‐19 severity [6, 19, 22, 42], and contribute to prolonged COVID‐associated gastrointestinal symptoms and cognitive impairment [33]. Similar to a previous report [43], our study identified that complements remain highly expressed 12 months after SARS‐CoV2 infection (Figure 4).

A limitation of this study is the small participant size, with the HC group including only 22 individuals. Although the age difference was not significant between hospitalized and convalescent patients, HC participants were slightly younger than other groups. Nevertheless, as bioinformatics analyses did not identify aging‐associated processes among the participant groups, this study provided valuable results regarding long COVID. We hope that our convalescent patient subtyping using both symptoms and the plasma proteome data will be validated in further studies.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study provided a plasma proteomic data set and identified symptom clusters in convalescent patients who had COVID‐19. Considering both symptoms and plasma protein expression levels, subtyping of convalescent patients with COVID‐19 may help each individual obtain suitable treatments. Since the patients were infected with SARS‐CoV‐2 only once, the results of this study avoided the interference of multiple infections and provided valuable data into long COVID. In future studies, we aim to recruit more participants to validate plasma protein expression patterns and subtypes of convalescent patients with COVID‐19.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: J.Y., L.R., and J.W. conceived and supervised the project. C.H., B.C., L.G., J.Z., X.W., L.R., and J.W. collected all the human plasma samples and clinical records. J.Y., J.L., Y.W., X.T., W.P., Q.W., T.D., X.G., H.Z., and Y.L. performed proteomic detection. J.Y., J.L., X.T., K.M., and Y.Y. performed bioinformatics analysis. J.Y. and J.L. wrote the manuscript with the input of all authors.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: This work was supported by grants from the National Key R&D Program of China (2023YFC2507102), the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences Innovation Fund for Medical Sciences, China (CIFMS2022‐I2M‐1‐011, CIFMS2022‐I2M‐2‐001, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐057, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐049, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐044, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐016, CIFMS2021‐I2M‐1‐001, 2022‐I2M‐CoV19‐003, and CIFMS2022‐I2M‐JB‐003), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82341064), the Haihe Laboratory of Cell Ecosystem Innovation Fund (22HHXBSS00008 and 22HHKYZX0034), and State Key Laboratory Special Fund 2060204.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The mass spectrometry proteomics data have been deposited to the ProteomeXchange Consortium (https://proteomecentral.proteomexchange.org) via the iProX partner repository with the data set identifier PXD054533 [44, 45].
